name: Begin Checkout
description: Counts the number of times users initiate the checkout process after adding one or more items to cart. This metric is a critical lower-funnel conversion indicator because it measures progression from shopping intent to purchase intent and helps identify friction between cart and checkout.
formula: Begin Checkout = COUNT of checkout initiation events where event_name = 'begin_checkout'
tags: checkout; funnel; e-commerce; conversion; purchase-journey; cart-to-checkout; lower-funnel
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Daily, Weekly, Monthly
sql_query: WITH begin_checkout_events AS ( SELECT event_date, event_timestamp, user_pseudo_id, session_id, event_name, transaction_currency AS currency, event_value_usd, ecommerce_value AS checkout_value FROM raw_digital_events WHERE event_name = 'begin_checkout' ) SELECT DATE(event_timestamp) AS metric_date, COUNT(*) AS begin_checkout FROM begin_checkout_events GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1;
calculation_notes: Count each begin_checkout event occurrence. Do not deduplicate unless a separate deduplicated version is explicitly required. Exclude bot/internal traffic where applicable. Ensure the event fires only once per checkout initiation and not on page refresh or step reload. If multiple begin_checkout events occur in one session due to restart behavior, each event should count unless business rules define unique session-level starts. For funnel reporting, align attribution to the event date/time and preserve item-level context.
business_use_case: Used by e-commerce, product, and CRO teams to monitor checkout funnel entry volume, evaluate cart-to-checkout progression, identify UX friction, measure impact of checkout experiments, and diagnose abandonment between cart and purchase.
source_data: Digital Analytics, Tag Management, Data Layer
dependencies: Cart page or cart state; begin_checkout event instrumentation; session_id; user identifier; item array; currency; checkout value
report_attributes: Date, Device Category, Channel, Source/Medium, Campaign, Landing Page, Country, Region, New vs Returning, Logged-in Status, Cart Value Bucket, Item Category, Item Brand, Coupon Code, Checkout Step
dashboard_usage: E-commerce Funnel Dashboard; Conversion Rate Dashboard; Checkout Performance Dashboard; Executive KPI Dashboard
derived_kpis: Cart-to-Checkout Rate; Checkout Abandonment Rate; Purchase Conversion Rate; Checkout Starts per User; Revenue per Checkout Start
related_metrics: Add to Cart; View Cart; Purchase; Checkout Completion Rate; Cart Abandonment Rate
aliases: Checkout Starts; Checkout Initiations; Started Checkout; Checkout Began

name: add to cart
description: Counts the number of times a user adds one or more products to the shopping cart. This metric is a key mid-funnel conversion indicator because it reflects purchase intent, product interest, and merchandising effectiveness before checkout begins.
formula: SUM(CASE WHEN event_name = 'add_to_cart' THEN COALESCE(quantity, 1) ELSE 0 END)
tags: cart; shopping cart; product intent; e-commerce funnel; merchandising; conversion; event tracking
aggregation_window: Event; Session; User; Daily; Weekly; Monthly
sql_query:
SELECT
  DATE(event_timestamp) AS event_date,
  COUNT(*) AS add_to_cart_events,
  SUM(COALESCE(quantity, 1)) AS add_to_cart,
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS users_with_add_to_cart,
  COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS sessions_with_add_to_cart
FROM raw_digital_events
WHERE event_name = 'add_to_cart'
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;
calculation_notes: Use the add_to_cart event only. The primary metric should sum item quantity when available; if quantity is missing, default to 1 per event. Exclude duplicate events caused by page reloads, tag firing retries, or server/client double collection by deduplicating on event_id where available. If multiple items are sent in one event, sum all item quantities. For product-level reporting, attribute counts to each item in the items array. For session or user rollups, count distinct sessions or users with at least one add_to_cart separately from total add-to-cart quantity. Exclude test traffic, internal traffic, and bot traffic. Ensure canceled cart actions are not netted out unless a separate net cart adds metric is defined.
business_use_case: Merchandising, product, and growth teams use this metric to evaluate product detail page effectiveness, promotional impact, pricing sensitivity, and traffic quality. It helps identify high-intent products, diagnose funnel drop-off before checkout, and compare campaign landing pages by downstream shopping behavior.
source_data: Digital Analytics; Tag Management System; Product Catalog
dependencies: add_to_cart event; item_id; quantity; session_id; user_id; event_timestamp; event_id; product dimensions; traffic source dimensions
report_attributes: Date, item_id, item_name, item_brand, item_category, item_variant, currency, device category, platform, page location, page type, session source, session medium, campaign, user type, country, region
dashboard_usage: E-commerce Funnel Dashboard; Product Performance Dashboard; Merchandising Dashboard; Campaign Landing Page Dashboard
derived_kpis: Cart-to-detail rate; Add-to-cart rate; Checkout initiation rate; Cart abandonment rate; Revenue per add-to-cart; Product conversion rate
related_metrics: view item; begin checkout; purchase; remove from cart; cart abandonment rate; item view-to-cart rate
aliases: ATC; add-to-basket; product list add; cart adds

name: Purchase
description: Counts completed purchase transactions captured in digital analytics. This metric measures how many orders were successfully placed and is a foundational conversion KPI for evaluating checkout effectiveness, campaign performance, merchandising impact, and revenue generation.
formula: Purchase = COUNT(DISTINCT transaction_id) for completed purchase events
tags: purchase; transaction; order; conversion; checkout; ecommerce; revenue; ga4; adobe
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Daily, Weekly, Monthly, Quarterly, Yearly
sql_query:
SELECT
  DATE(event_timestamp) AS event_date,
  COUNT(DISTINCT transaction_id) AS purchase
FROM raw_events
WHERE event_name = 'purchase'
  AND transaction_id IS NOT NULL
  AND COALESCE(event_status, 'completed') = 'completed'
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;
calculation_notes: Deduplicate by transaction_id to avoid double-counting from page reloads, duplicate tags, server/client dual collection, or retries. Include only completed purchase events and exclude test orders, internal traffic, canceled transactions logged as purchases, and zero-value sandbox transactions unless business rules explicitly include them. If transaction_id is unavailable, use a fallback deduplication key such as order_id plus user_pseudo_id plus event_date, but this is less reliable. Refunds should not reduce this metric; track them separately. For multi-currency implementations, purchase count is unaffected, but ensure transaction completion logic is consistent across regions.
business_use_case: Used by e-commerce, growth, product, and executive teams to monitor sales volume, evaluate checkout funnel performance, compare channel quality, assess merchandising and pricing changes, and forecast demand. It is also a core input to conversion rate, average order value, revenue per user, and campaign ROI reporting.
source_data: Digital Analytics; E-commerce Platform; Order Management System; CRM
dependencies: purchase event; transaction_id; event_timestamp; order completion status; traffic source dimensions; product dimensions
report_attributes: Date,Transaction ID,User ID,Session ID,Device Category,Platform,Country,Region,City,Source,Medium,Campaign,Default Channel Group,Landing Page,Page Type,Product ID,Product Name,Product Category,Coupon,Payment Type,Currency
dashboard_usage: Executive KPI Dashboard; E-commerce Performance Dashboard; Checkout Funnel Dashboard; Marketing Channel Dashboard; Merchandising Dashboard
derived_kpis: Conversion Rate; Average Order Value; Revenue; Revenue Per User; Cart-to-Purchase Rate; Checkout Completion Rate; ROAS
related_metrics: Revenue; Transactions; Average Order Value; Checkout Starts; Add to Cart; Conversion Rate; Refunds
aliases: Transactions; Orders; Completed Orders; Sales; Purchase Count

name: Product View
description: Counts the number of times a product detail page or product detail view is displayed to a user. This metric is foundational for understanding product interest, merchandising effectiveness, and the top of the commerce conversion funnel. It helps teams evaluate product discovery, traffic quality, and the relationship between product exposure and downstream actions such as add-to-cart, checkout, and purchase.
formula: Product View = COUNT of product detail view events where event_name = 'view_item' (or equivalent product detail view event) and product_id is present
tags: product; product detail page; pdp; ecommerce; merchandising; funnel; engagement; ga4; adobe analytics
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Daily, Weekly, Monthly
sql_query:
SELECT
  DATE(event_timestamp) AS event_date,
  COUNT(*) AS product_view
FROM raw_digital_events
WHERE event_name IN ('view_item', 'product_view', 'prodView')
  AND COALESCE(product_id, JSON_VALUE(event_params, '$.ecommerce.items[0].item_id')) IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;
calculation_notes: Count each qualifying product detail view event as one product view. If a single event contains multiple items, only count all items as separate product views if the implementation intentionally supports multi-item product detail impressions; otherwise for standard PDP implementations count one event per viewed product. Exclude non-product pages, quick-view overlays unless the business explicitly defines quick view as a product view, bot/internal traffic, QA environments, and duplicate events fired on page refresh or SPA rerenders. Ensure product_id is populated and stable across platforms. For session-level reporting, multiple views of the same product in the same session should generally be counted unless a separate unique product views metric is required.
business_use_case: Merchandising teams use this metric to identify high-interest products and optimize assortment placement. UX and product teams use it to assess PDP traffic and navigation effectiveness. Marketing teams compare product views by campaign, channel, and audience to evaluate traffic quality. Analysts use it as the denominator for add-to-cart rate, product conversion rate, and purchase funnel diagnostics.
source_data: Digital Analytics; Tag Management System; Product Catalog
dependencies: Product detail view event; product_id; product_name; page_type; timestamp; session_id; user_id; traffic source dimensions
report_attributes: date, product_id, product_name, item_brand, item_category, item_variant, page_type, device_category, platform, source, medium, campaign, channel_group, country, region, session_id, user_id
dashboard_usage: E-commerce Executive Dashboard; Product Performance Dashboard; Merchandising Dashboard; Conversion Funnel Dashboard; Campaign Landing Performance Dashboard
derived_kpis: Add-to-Cart Rate; Product Detail Conversion Rate; Purchase Rate per Product View; Revenue per Product View; Product Bounce Proxy; PDP Engagement Rate
related_metrics: Add to Cart; Item List View; Item Click; Checkout Start; Purchase; Product Detail Conversion Rate; Revenue per Product View
aliases: PDP Views; Product Detail Views; Item Views; Product Page Views

name: Checkout Completion Rate
description: The percentage of checkout starts that result in a completed purchase within the same session or attributed conversion window. This metric measures the effectiveness of the checkout funnel and helps identify friction in payment, shipping, form completion, or technical performance that prevents users from finishing a transaction.
formula: (Number of completed purchases / Number of checkout starts) * 100
tags: checkout; funnel; conversion; e-commerce; purchase; cart; order-completion; cro
sql_query:
WITH checkout_starts AS (
  SELECT
    session_id,
    MIN(event_timestamp) AS checkout_start_ts
  FROM raw_events
  WHERE event_name = 'begin_checkout'
  GROUP BY session_id
), purchases AS (
  SELECT
    session_id,
    MIN(event_timestamp) AS purchase_ts
  FROM raw_events
  WHERE event_name = 'purchase'
  GROUP BY session_id
), joined AS (
  SELECT
    c.session_id,
    c.checkout_start_ts,
    p.purchase_ts,
    CASE
      WHEN p.purchase_ts IS NOT NULL AND p.purchase_ts >= c.checkout_start_ts THEN 1
      ELSE 0
    END AS completed_checkout
  FROM checkout_starts c
  LEFT JOIN purchases p
    ON c.session_id = p.session_id
)
SELECT
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(completed_checkout) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0), 2) AS checkout_completion_rate
FROM joined;
calculation_notes: Use checkout starts as the denominator, not cart views. Count one checkout start per session unless business rules require counting every distinct checkout attempt. Count a purchase only if it occurs after the checkout start. Exclude canceled, failed, test, fraudulent, or duplicate orders where possible. For cross-session attribution, an alternate version may use user-level conversion within a defined attribution window, but the standard definition should remain session-based for funnel analysis. If multiple purchases occur in one session after one checkout start, count the session as one completed checkout unless analyzing multi-order behavior separately.
business_use_case: E-commerce, product, UX, and CRO teams use this metric to monitor checkout funnel health, identify abandonment points, evaluate payment and shipping changes, compare device performance, and prioritize fixes that improve order completion.
source_data: Digital Analytics, E-commerce Platform, Order Management System
dependencies: begin_checkout event; purchase event; session_id; event_timestamp; order status filter; bot/test traffic exclusion rules
report_attributes: Date, Device Category, Traffic Source, Default Channel Group, Campaign, Landing Page, Checkout Step, Payment Method, Shipping Method, Country, Region, New vs Returning User, Browser, Operating System
dashboard_usage: Executive KPI Dashboard; E-commerce Conversion Dashboard; Checkout Funnel Dashboard; CRO Performance Dashboard
derived_kpis: Checkout Abandonment Rate; Revenue per Checkout Start; Payment Step Completion Rate; Shipping Step Completion Rate
related_metrics: Cart-to-Checkout Rate; Purchase Conversion Rate; Checkout Abandonment Rate; Cart Abandonment Rate; Average Order Value
aliases: checkout conversion rate; checkout success rate; order completion rate; purchase completion rate

name: Cart Removals
description: Counts the number of times an item is removed from the shopping cart. This metric helps quantify cart abandonment behavior at the product-interaction level, identify friction in pricing or shipping expectations, and evaluate merchandising, promotion, and checkout experience effectiveness.
formula: Cart Removals = COUNT(remove_from_cart events) where each qualifying event represents a user action that decreases cart quantity or removes a line item from the cart.
tags: cart; checkout; ecommerce; abandonment; product interaction; funnel; ga4; adobe analytics
sql_query: WITH base AS ( SELECT event_date, event_timestamp, user_pseudo_id, session_id, event_name, COALESCE(items.item_id, item_id) AS item_id, COALESCE(items.item_name, item_name) AS item_name, COALESCE(items.quantity, quantity, 1) AS quantity FROM raw_digital_events LEFT JOIN UNNEST(items) AS items ON TRUE WHERE event_name = 'remove_from_cart' ) SELECT DATE(event_timestamp) AS metric_date, COUNT(*) AS cart_removals, SUM(COALESCE(quantity,1)) AS units_removed, COUNT(DISTINCT user_pseudo_id) AS users_with_cart_removals, COUNT(DISTINCT CONCAT(CAST(user_pseudo_id AS STRING),'-',CAST(session_id AS STRING))) AS sessions_with_cart_removals FROM base GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1;
calculation_notes: Use the remove_from_cart event only; do not infer removals from cart quantity snapshots unless event tracking is unavailable. If one event contains multiple items, COUNT(*) returns event count while SUM(quantity) returns units removed; align reporting definition before implementation. Exclude system-generated cart resets, expired-cart cleanups, and duplicate events caused by page reloads or retry logic. For server-side implementations, deduplicate using event_id where available. If quantity is reduced from 3 to 1, record quantity removed as 2 when supported; otherwise count as one removal event. Ensure bot and internal traffic filters are applied.
business_use_case: Merchandising teams use this metric to identify products frequently removed after being added, signaling pricing, shipping, stock, or product-detail issues. UX and CRO teams use it to diagnose checkout friction and test cart design changes. Marketing teams compare removals by campaign or promotion to assess traffic quality. Product and analytics teams monitor the add-to-cart to remove-from-cart relationship as an early warning indicator of conversion leakage.
source_data: Digital Analytics, Tag Management, E-commerce Platform
dependencies: remove_from_cart event; item_id; item_name; quantity; session_id; user identifier; event_timestamp; traffic source dimensions; bot filtering
report_attributes: Date, Product ID, Product Name, Brand, Category, Variant, Quantity Removed, User Type, Device Category, Platform, Session Source, Session Medium, Campaign, Country, Cart ID
dashboard_usage: E-commerce Funnel Dashboard; Cart Behavior Dashboard; Product Performance Dashboard; Conversion Optimization Dashboard
derived_kpis: Cart Removal Rate; Add-to-Remove Ratio; Net Cart Adds; Checkout Drop-off Rate by Product
related_metrics: Add to Cart; Cart Views; Checkout Starts; Purchase Conversion Rate; Cart Abandonment Rate; Units Removed from Cart
aliases: Removed from Cart; Cart Item Removals; Remove from Cart Events; Basket Removals

name: Cart Views
description: Counts the number of times users view the shopping cart page or cart overlay. This metric indicates purchase intent and helps evaluate progression from product consideration to checkout. It is useful for identifying cart engagement trends, merchandising effectiveness, and friction before checkout.
tags: cart; checkout funnel; purchase intent; ecommerce; shopping cart; funnel analysis
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Daily, Weekly, Monthly
sql_query:
SELECT DATE(event_timestamp) AS event_date, COUNT(*) AS cart_views
FROM raw_digital_events
WHERE event_name = 'view_cart'
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;
calculation_notes: Count each fired cart view event once. Deduplicate if instrumentation can fire multiple times on a single page load or SPA route change using event_id, session_id + timestamp proximity, or page_view sequence logic. Include both full cart page views and cart drawer/modal views only if the business defines both as cart exposure; otherwise restrict to page_type = 'cart'. Exclude internal traffic, QA environments, bots, and monitoring tools. If using Adobe, ensure event25 is reserved consistently for cart views. For multi-currency implementations, currency does not affect the count but should be captured for context.
business_use_case: E-commerce, merchandising, UX, and conversion teams use this metric to monitor how many shoppers reach the cart, evaluate the impact of product page and add-to-cart changes, identify abandonment points before checkout, and assess whether cart UX changes improve progression to checkout start and purchase.
dependencies: view_cart event; session_id; user_id or pseudo_user_id; event_timestamp; page_type; item_id; environment filter; bot filter
report_attributes: Date, Device Category, Source/Medium, Campaign, Landing Page, Page Type, User Type, Country, Region, Product Category, Item Brand, Session Default Channel Group, Cart Type
dashboard_usage: E-commerce Funnel Dashboard; Checkout Performance Dashboard; Merchandising Dashboard; Executive KPI Dashboard
derived_kpis: Cart-to-Checkout Rate; Cart Abandonment Rate; Cart Views per User; Purchase Conversion Rate from Cart Viewers
related_metrics: Add to Carts; Checkout Starts; Purchases; Cart Abandonments; Sessions with Cart View
aliases: Shopping Cart Views; View Cart Events; Cart Page Views

name: Checkouts
description: The total number of checkout initiation events, representing how often users begin the checkout process after adding items to cart. This metric is critical for evaluating purchase intent, identifying funnel progression from cart to payment, and diagnosing friction before transaction completion.
formula: Checkouts = COUNT of checkout initiation events where event_name = 'begin_checkout'
tags: checkout; funnel; e-commerce; purchase-intent; cart; conversion; ga4; adobe
sql_query:
SELECT
  DATE(event_timestamp) AS event_date,
  COUNT(*) AS checkouts
FROM raw_events
WHERE event_name = 'begin_checkout'
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;
calculation_notes: Count each valid checkout start event once per firing. If instrumentation can fire multiple begin_checkout events in the same session for the same cart, consider deduplicating by user_pseudo_id + session_id + cart_id + event_date_time bucket. Exclude internal traffic, QA environments, bots, and test orders where applicable. For multi-step checkout implementations, this metric should only count the first checkout initiation step, not subsequent shipping/payment step views. If cart edits return users to checkout and the business wants unique checkout starts, create a companion unique-checkouts metric at user-session-cart grain.
business_use_case: E-commerce, merchandising, and UX teams use this metric to monitor how effectively carts convert into checkout starts, assess the impact of promotions or cart-page changes, and identify abandonment between cart and purchase. Paid media teams may also compare traffic sources by checkout propensity to optimize acquisition quality.
dependencies: begin_checkout event; session_id; user_pseudo_id or visitor_id; cart_id; item_id; currency; value; internal traffic filter
report_attributes: Date, Device Category, Channel Group, Source/Medium, Campaign, Landing Page, Country, Region, Product Category, Cart Value Band, New vs Returning User
dashboard_usage: E-commerce Funnel Dashboard; Conversion Performance Dashboard; Checkout Optimization Dashboard; Executive KPI Dashboard
derived_kpis: Checkout-to-Purchase Rate; Cart-to-Checkout Rate; Checkout Abandonment Rate; Revenue per Checkout
related_metrics: Add to Carts; Purchases; Cart Abandonment Rate; Checkout Completion Rate; Average Order Value
aliases: Checkout Starts; Begin Checkout; Checkout Initiations

name: Units
description: The total number of individual product or service units sold during the selected reporting period. This metric matters because it reflects sales volume, supports demand forecasting, inventory planning, merchandising analysis, and helps distinguish whether revenue changes are driven by price, mix, or quantity.
formula: SUM(quantity) for all completed purchase line items within the reporting period, excluding cancelled, fully refunded, and test transactions unless explicitly included by business rules.
tags: sales; quantity; volume; orders; products; ecommerce; inventory; commerce
aggregation_window: Event, Daily, Weekly, Monthly, Quarterly, Yearly
sql_query:
SELECT
  DATE(event_timestamp) AS event_date,
  SUM(COALESCE(item.quantity, 0)) AS units
FROM raw_events e
CROSS JOIN UNNEST(e.items) AS item
WHERE e.event_name = 'purchase'
  AND COALESCE(e.is_test, FALSE) = FALSE
  AND COALESCE(e.order_status, 'completed') NOT IN ('cancelled', 'refunded')
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;
calculation_notes: Count item quantities from purchase events at the line-item level, not order count. Deduplicate transactions using transaction_id or purchaseID if the implementation can resend purchase events. Exclude cancelled orders and optionally subtract refunded quantities if reporting net units instead of gross units. For partial refunds, reduce only the refunded line-item quantities. Ensure bundle logic is defined consistently: count parent bundle only, child components only, or both, depending on merchandising standards.
business_use_case: Merchandising, finance, and operations teams use Units to evaluate product demand, monitor sales trends, forecast inventory, assess promotion effectiveness, and compare category performance independent of pricing changes.
source_data: Digital Analytics, ERP, Order Management System
dependencies: purchase event; transaction_id; item_id; quantity; order_status; refund event or refund status; test traffic flag
report_attributes: Date, Product ID, Product Name, Product Category, Brand, Variant, Channel, Device, Campaign, Region, Currency, Transaction ID
dashboard_usage: Executive Commerce Overview; Product Performance Dashboard; Inventory And Demand Planning Dashboard; Campaign Performance Dashboard
derived_kpis: Average Units Per Transaction; Units Per User; Product Mix Share; Net Units Sold; Sell-Through Rate
related_metrics: Orders; Revenue; Average Order Value; Item Revenue; Quantity Refunded
aliases: unit sales; items sold; quantity sold; total units; units sold

name: Units per transaction
description: Average number of units purchased in each completed transaction. This metric indicates basket size at the item level and helps teams understand purchasing behavior, merchandising effectiveness, bundling performance, and the impact of promotions on order composition.
formula: Units per transaction = Total units sold / Total transactions
tags: basket size; average order composition; items per order; transaction efficiency; merchandising; e-commerce; retail
aggregation_window: Event, Daily, Weekly, Monthly, Quarterly, Yearly
sql_query:
WITH purchase_events AS (
  SELECT
    event_date,
    transaction_id,
    items
  FROM raw_digital_analytics_events
  WHERE event_name = 'purchase'
    AND transaction_id IS NOT NULL
), transaction_units AS (
  SELECT
    transaction_id,
    SUM(COALESCE(CAST(item.quantity AS NUMERIC), 0)) AS units_in_transaction
  FROM purchase_events pe
  CROSS JOIN UNNEST(pe.items) AS item
  GROUP BY transaction_id
)
SELECT
  SAFE_DIVIDE(SUM(units_in_transaction), COUNT(DISTINCT transaction_id)) AS units_per_transaction
FROM transaction_units;
calculation_notes: Use completed purchase events only. Count distinct transaction_id values to avoid duplicate purchase hits. Sum item quantity across all items in a transaction; if quantity is missing, default to 0 rather than 1 unless business rules explicitly define otherwise. Exclude cancelled, refunded, test, or fraudulent orders unless the reporting standard requires net fulfilled transactions. For partial refunds, do not reduce this metric unless calculating a separate net units per transaction KPI. Ensure bundled products are handled consistently: either count bundle parent only or all child units based on merchandising policy.
business_use_case: Merchandising, e-commerce, and growth teams use this metric to evaluate cross-sell and upsell effectiveness, product bundling strategies, cart-building initiatives, and promotional mechanics such as buy-more-save-more offers. It is also used to compare basket composition by channel, device, campaign, category, and customer segment.
source_data: Digital Analytics; E-commerce Platform; Order Management System; ERP
dependencies: purchase event; transaction_id; item quantity; product/item array; order status filter
report_attributes: Date,Transaction ID,Product SKU,Product Name,Product Category,Brand,Channel,Source/Medium,Campaign,Device Category,Region,Customer Type
dashboard_usage: Executive commerce KPI dashboard; Merchandising performance dashboard; Conversion and basket analysis dashboard; Campaign performance dashboard
derived_kpis: Average items per order by channel; Average items per order by campaign; Basket size index; Units per purchasing user
related_metrics: Average order value; Transactions; Units sold; Revenue per transaction; Cart-to-detail rate
aliases: items per transaction; units per order; average items per order; average units per transaction

name: Average order value
description: Measures the average revenue generated per completed order over a defined period. It is a core commerce efficiency metric used to understand basket size, pricing effectiveness, merchandising performance, and the impact of promotions, bundles, cross-sell, and upsell strategies.
formula: Average Order Value = Total Revenue / Total Number of Orders
tags: aov; average order value; order revenue; basket size; e-commerce; monetization; commerce; kpi
aggregation_window: Event, Daily, Weekly, Monthly, Quarterly, Yearly
sql_query:
WITH purchase_orders AS (
  SELECT
    COALESCE(transaction_id, order_id) AS order_id,
    event_date,
    currency,
    revenue
  FROM raw_digital_analytics_events
  WHERE event_name = 'purchase'
    AND COALESCE(transaction_id, order_id) IS NOT NULL
    AND revenue IS NOT NULL
), deduped_orders AS (
  SELECT
    order_id,
    MIN(event_date) AS order_date,
    MAX(currency) AS currency,
    MAX(revenue) AS order_revenue
  FROM purchase_orders
  GROUP BY order_id
)
SELECT
  SUM(order_revenue) / NULLIF(COUNT(order_id), 0) AS average_order_value
FROM deduped_orders;
calculation_notes: Use completed purchase events only. Deduplicate by unique order identifier to avoid inflating both revenue and order count from duplicate tracking, retries, or server/client dual collection. Revenue should align to the business definition of order revenue, typically item revenue after item-level discounts and before or after tax/shipping depending on finance policy; keep the definition consistent across reporting. Exclude canceled, fully refunded, test, fraudulent, and internal employee orders unless the reporting purpose explicitly includes them. If multiple currencies exist, convert to a reporting currency before aggregation using the transaction-date FX rate. Partial refunds should not retroactively change AOV unless the organization defines a net AOV variant.
business_use_case: Merchandising teams use AOV to evaluate bundle and cross-sell programs. Marketing teams use it to assess traffic quality and campaign profitability. Product and UX teams monitor whether checkout, cart, and recommendation changes increase basket size. Finance and BI teams use it in revenue forecasting and contribution margin analysis.
source_data: Digital Analytics, ERP, Order Management System, Finance
dependencies: purchase event; transaction_id/order_id; revenue/value; currency; order status; refund/cancellation flags
report_attributes: Date, Device category, Channel, Source/Medium, Campaign, Landing page, Product category, Brand, Coupon code, Country, Region, Currency, New vs Returning customer
dashboard_usage: Executive Revenue Dashboard; E-commerce Performance Dashboard; Marketing Channel Dashboard; Merchandising Dashboard
derived_kpis: Revenue per user; Revenue per session; Gross merchandise value; Customer lifetime value; Average revenue per paying user
related_metrics: Orders; Revenue; Conversion rate; Cart-to-purchase rate; Units per transaction; Gross margin
aliases: AOV; average basket value; average transaction value; average order revenue

name: Internal Searches
description: Counts the number of on-site search actions performed by users within a digital property. This metric indicates how often visitors rely on internal search to find content, products, services, or information, and helps identify discoverability issues, content demand, and navigation gaps.
formula: Internal Searches = Count of search events where a valid internal search term is submitted
tags: site search; internal search; search behavior; navigation; discoverability; content demand; product discovery
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Manufacturing; Other
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Daily, Weekly, Monthly
sql_query:
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('day', event_timestamp) AS metric_date,
  COUNT(*) AS internal_searches
FROM raw_digital_events
WHERE event_name IN ('view_search_results', 'site_search', 'internal_search')
  AND COALESCE(TRIM(search_term), '') <> ''
  AND COALESCE(search_type, 'internal') = 'internal'
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;
calculation_notes: Count one search per qualifying search event submission. Exclude blank, null, whitespace-only, and system-generated terms. If the implementation fires both search submit and search results events for the same action, deduplicate using search_id or a session-level combination of user/session/search_term/timestamp within a short threshold. Refined searches should count as separate searches if a new term is submitted. Searches triggered by autocomplete selection may be included if they lead to a search results view and are consistently tracked. Exclude external search engines and support-vendor hosted search unless explicitly defined as internal search.
business_use_case: Product, UX, content, merchandising, and analytics teams use this metric to evaluate reliance on search, identify unmet content or product discovery needs, optimize navigation and taxonomy, prioritize zero-result query fixes, and assess the impact of search experience changes on downstream conversion and engagement.
source_data: Digital Analytics, Search Platform Logs, Tag Management System
dependencies: Search event tracking; Search term parameter; Session ID; User ID or pseudo ID; Timestamp; Search type dimension; Optional search_id for deduplication
report_attributes: Date, Search Term, Search Results Count, Search Type, Search Method, Device Category, Platform, Page Type, Landing Page, Session Source/Medium, Campaign, Country, Region, User Type
dashboard_usage: Digital Analytics Executive Dashboard; Search Performance Dashboard; Content Discovery Dashboard; E-commerce Merchandising Dashboard; UX Optimization Dashboard
derived_kpis: Search Usage Rate; Search Exit Rate; Search Refinement Rate; Zero Results Search Rate; Search Conversion Rate; Revenue per Search
related_metrics: Search Users; Search Sessions; Search Usage Rate; Zero Results Searches; Search Refinements; Search Exits; Search Conversion Rate; Average Search Depth
aliases: On-site Searches; Site Searches; Internal Site Search Count; Search Events
